primary_diagnosis.name: Toxic shock syndrome (staphylococcal, menstrual/tampon-associated) with distributive shock

pathophysiology_summary: A toxigenic strain of Staphylococcus aureus (classically colonising a retained high-absorbency tampon) elaborates the superantigen TSST-1 (or, for the streptococcal form, Streptococcus pyogenes exotoxins). Superantigens bind MHC class II and T-cell receptors outside the antigen groove, triggering massive polyclonal T-cell activation and a cytokine storm (TNF, IL-1, IL-2). The result is diffuse capillary leak and vasodilation producing distributive (vasodilatory) shock, a diffuse macular 'sunburn' erythroderma, mucosal hyperaemia, high fever, myalgia, vomiting/diarrhoea and, as perfusion fails, altered mental status and progressive multi-organ dysfunction (renal and hepatic). Desquamation of the palms and soles is a late feature (1–2 weeks) and is absent acutely. Management is time-critical: aggressive intravenous fluid resuscitation for the shock, source control (removal of the tampon), and antibiotics including a protein-synthesis inhibitor (clindamycin/lincosamide) to switch off toxin production plus anti-staphylococcal cover — all delivered in hospital/ICU. Delay leads to irreversible multi-organ failure and death.

differential.diagnosis: Toxic shock syndrome / toxin-mediated distributive shock
differential.evidence_basis: High fever with a diffuse sunburn-like erythroderma AND near-syncope on sitting up points to a systemic toxin-mediated process with shock, not a skin complaint.
Fever plus shock plus altered mental status is sepsis until proven otherwise; the erythroderma and mucosal changes point toward the toxin-mediated subtype.
A febrile widespread rash can be viral, but profound hypotension, severe vomiting/diarrhoea and rapid deterioration are not features of a simple viral exanthem.

differential.diagnosis: Sunburn / benign cause of red skin
differential.evidence_basis: The patient's own 'sunburn' framing is a red herring — sunburn does not cause high fever, shock, confusion or GI losses. Anchoring here would be a fatal error.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At the opening the system must escalate the febrile-rash-plus-near-syncope picture as a shock emergency and resist the benign 'sunburn' anchor.

differential.evidence_basis: Menstruation with a retained high-absorbency tampon, diffuse macular erythroderma, mucosal hyperaemia, fever, GI symptoms and shock is the classic toxic shock constellation.
Also causes distributive shock, but the 24-hour onset, high fever, and flat macular (non-urticarial) rash argue strongly against it.
Another cause of fever with rash and shock; distinguished here by a diffuse blanching macular erythema rather than a non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash — but it must be actively excluded.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Eliciting menstruation + tampon use sharply raises the pre-test probability of the menstrual staphylococcal form and points to a source-control action the patient can perform.

differential.diagnosis: Toxic shock syndrome (staphylococcal, tampon-associated) with distributive shock and early multi-organ dysfunction
differential.evidence_basis: Classic tetrad (diffuse erythroderma, hypotension, high fever, mucosal hyperaemia) with a plausible focus (retained tampon); confirmation requires hospital cultures and organ-function bloods — cannot be confirmed remotely.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Telehealth cannot confirm the diagnosis; the correct end-state is emergency escalation and time-critical source control, not a home management plan.

red_flags.description: Profound hypotension with near-syncope on sitting/standing — distributive shock
red_flags.clinical_significance: Vasodilatory shock; organ hypoperfusion is under way and requires immediate fluid resuscitation and ambulance transport.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Untreated distributive shock progresses to cardiovascular collapse and death within hours.

red_flags.description: Altered mental status / new confusion and drowsiness
red_flags.clinical_significance: Marker of cerebral hypoperfusion and evolving multi-organ dysfunction — a time-critical deterioration sign.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Confusion heralds decompensation; failure to escalate risks obtundation, airway compromise and arrest.

red_flags.description: High fever with a diffuse macular erythroderma and mucosal hyperaemia
red_flags.clinical_significance: The toxin-mediated toxidrome signature; separates this from a benign rash or a simple viral exanthem.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Anchoring on 'sunburn' or a viral rash delays recognition of a systemic emergency.

red_flags.description: Retained high-absorbency tampon during menstruation as the likely toxin source
red_flags.clinical_significance: Identifies the removable infective focus; source control (tampon removal) is a definitive, patient-performable step alongside escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Leaving the tampon in situ allows continued toxin production and worsening shock.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Taking the patient's 'like a sunburn' description at face value and treating this as a dermatological or trivial problem, missing the shock.
Attributing the vomiting and watery diarrhoea to viral gastro and advising oral rehydration at home, ignoring the fever, rash and hypotension.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Failure to take a menstrual/tampon history
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Not asking about menstruation and internal sanitary products, thereby missing the removable source and the specific diagnosis.
Recognising the patient is unwell but arranging same-day GP or urgent care rather than an immediate 000 ambulance for shock with altered mental status.

risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Altered mentation + hypotension present (2 of 3 qSOFA criteria on reported features)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Two or more markers indicate high risk of poor outcome and mandate emergency resuscitation and escalation.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Measured blood pressure and heart rate (reported BP 80/45, HR 135 are clinician-measured shock vitals, not obtainable in this chat)
Respiratory rate and oxygen saturation on a clinical monitor (RR 24, SpO2 95%)
Examination confirmation of conjunctival and oropharyngeal hyperaemia / 'strawberry tongue'
Isolation of Staphylococcus aureus or Streptococcus pyogenes from a focal site (e.g. vaginal swab culture)
Bloods showing multi-organ dysfunction (elevated creatinine and transaminases)
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Call 000 immediately for a Priority 1 ambulance for a young adult in distributive shock with altered mental status; while waiting, sensitively instruct removal of the retained high-absorbency tampon (source control), lay her flat with legs elevated, keep her warm, and monitor breathing and responsiveness continuously.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: The specific label cannot be confirmed remotely and does not need to be — the risk is UNDER-action (anchoring on sunburn/viral rash/gastro and failing to escalate the shock), not naming the exact syndrome.

guidelines_referenced.reference: CDC Toxic Shock Syndrome (Staphylococcal) 2011 Case Definition
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Fever, diffuse macular erythroderma, hypotension and multisystem (>=3 organ) involvement with later desquamation define staphylococcal TSS; a retained tampon is a classic menstrual risk factor.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (Australia) — Sepsis and septic shock
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Distributive shock requires immediate large-volume IV crystalloid resuscitation, urgent source control, and early antibiotics; escalate emergently.
